title: Has anyone made their own backtesting software?
author: Minimum-Lychee7812
selftext: I’m trying to make my own trading backtesting software. I have no programming background at all, so I’m just trying to use the ai-app builders for now. Has anyone tried this before? Or made their own working version? It’s a little bit hard for me to throw money on fxreplay knowing that my strategy might not work, so that’s why I’m building my own.

comments.author: StrategyTune
comments.body: Yes, here is a free manual backtesting app [https://strategytune.com/app](https://strategytune.com/app)

comments.author: jtm_ind
comments.body: I have. One thing to ensure you include is accounting for randomness when you send a buy/sell order, make it adjustable so that you can view the same Strategy under different conditions. And don’t forget commissions

replies.author: jtm_ind
replies.body: I started using back trader in python, then I took an algo trading course to understand the math & nuance. That’s when I picked up on accounting for randomness, commissions etc. when I built my own  back testing software I tested it heavily against back trader to make sure the numbers matched.Once they did I added different account types like CASH postition sizing and now I am working on a risk management suite

comments.author: FortuneGrouchy4701
comments.body:
I have my own. For a long time and is not AI made. That I can’t share but feel free to ask what you want. 
Look for HFTBacktest. Is a nice one make with rust and python. HFT.

replies.author: HumansHaveUrges
replies.body: I have my own hft back tester too. My only concern is that I can’t model fill because I only have live L1 data. I wonder how you get around that

replies.author: FortuneGrouchy4701
replies.body:
I have L3 Data in general book with 30 levels is fine. HFTBacktest has some fill models, [https://hftbacktest.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorials/Probability%20Queue%20Models.html](https://hftbacktest.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorials/Probability%20Queue%20Models.html) . 

We use some maths on the company that take care of that part using the book and trades data combined with statistics.

replies.author: HumansHaveUrges
replies.body: I can’t use that python backtester because it needs me to input l2 data which I don’t have. L2 data is like $1500 a month

replies.author: FortuneGrouchy4701
replies.body: I have some old data if you want.

replies.author: HumansHaveUrges
replies.body: I’m going to test with live fill first. PDT will change from $25k to $3k soon

comments.author: Chemical_Badger6227
comments.body:
Built my own in Python. Even with a programming background, AI coding assistants (Claude, Cursor, etc.) are a massive accelerator — they're great at churning out the boilerplate: data loading, position tracking, P&L calculation. You focus on the logic, they handle the plumbing. Way better than AI app builders because you actually understand and own the code.

But here's the thing nobody tells beginners: building the backtester is the easy part. Validating that your results aren't garbage is the hard part.

Things I wish I'd known from day one:

1. Walk-forward testing — split your data into chunks, optimise on chunk 1, test on chunk 2, optimize on 1+2, test on 3, etc. If your strategy only works in-sample, it's curve-fitted junk.

2. Shuffle/baseline test — run your strategy against random entry signals. If random entries produce similar returns, your "edge" is just market beta.

3. Commissions and slippage — jtm\_ind nailed this. A strategy that looks great at 0 fees often dies at realistic costs. Model maker/taker fees separately.

4. Don't optimize too many parameters — every parameter you add is a degree of freedom to overfit. A strategy with 2-3 params that works across multiple assets is worth 10x more than a 15-param monster that works on one ticker.

The visual replay tools (FX Replay, StrategyTune) are fine for discretionary testing, but if you want to test hundreds of parameter combinations or run statistical validation, you need code. Python + pandas + an AI assistant gets you there faster than you'd think.

replies.body: Thanks for the advice! Really appreciate it!

comments.author: Denis_Kurilchik
comments.body:
Built my own in Python a few years ago before decent AI coding tools existed. Would do it very differently now.

Without a programming background, Claude or Cursor can write most of the logic for you and iterate fast. The hard parts to get right regardless of tool: account for commissions, slippage (0.05-0.1% for crypto), and make sure you dont have lookahead bias (peeking at future candles to make past decisions). That one kills a lot of backtests that look great on paper.

Start with one asset, one timeframe, one rule.

comments.author: jabberw0ckee
comments.body:
Yes. You can use an api like AlphaVantage or FMP, a place to store the data and a script to walk the candles to test a strategy. 

A spreadsheet or a database can be used to store the candles. 

Google apps scripts or your computer or the cloud can host your code. 

Good luck!

replies.body: It works on the free version but only for certain timeframes. You cannot go below the daily for backtesting

comments.author: woztrades
comments.body: We built one for prediction markets.. thing is it's hard to store historic orderbook information to provide accurate results, and if you have a strategy that relies on some kind of edge (like price swings from correlated assets), you've got to store that historic info as well.

comments.author: Realistic-Falcon4998
comments.body: Yes.Let me drop a thought here. Backtesting is actually a great way of testing your app performance. What you see is often a little less than what you get in the actual trades. But, be careful not to overfit your bot should you notice some losses in the backtest. Instead, focus on hardening the strategy. For example, should you find a pattern underperforming, say Hammer, blacklisting it won't reduce your losses. In fact, a well balanced strategy is better than an aggressive one.🙂

comments.author: ElectronicTonicWater
comments.body: I built my own version, which is an MCP server that plugs into Claude and it gives it the ability to run SQL queries over the stock universe. I also had some success running a prompt instructing it to backtest a setup.

comments.author: pickupandplay22
comments.body:
Yeah, I built one. Took the opposite approach to most of the tools in this thread: instead of building your own from scratch, you paste a strategy in plain English and get a verdict against 3 years of real Binance data with actual fees in under 2 minutes. No code, no signup, no payment, 10 tests per hour.

Reason I built it: same problem you describe. I had ideas, didn't want to pay $20-50/mo for visual replay tools to validate them, and most backtesters hide what's actually being tested.

Things that took me the longest to figure out, whether you build your own or use mine:

\- Walk-forward validation, not just train/test split. Most strategies that look great on a 70/30 split fall apart when you roll the window forward and retrain.

\- Slippage and fees per fill, not deducted at the end. A 1% per-trade strategy at 10bps fees and $0.001 slippage can be breakeven.

\- Multiple comparisons math. If you test 100 variants, the "best one" looks like Sharpe 2.0 by chance alone. Deflated Sharpe Ratio adjusts for it. Saved me from chasing a few flukes.

\- Regime tagging. Same strategy can print in low-vol ranges and get vaporized in trending high-vol. Tag every trade with its regime, you'll see it.

If you want to test ideas before committing to building, mine is at stratproof.com. Crypto only right now, no fees, no signup, you can validate the concept in 5 minutes. If you decide to build anyway, those four points are the ones that ate the most of my time.
